audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 1 Samuel 12, first-pass restored draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: Reviewed for Samuel's public accountability, sons' corruption distinction, historical theology, Bedan variant, king-and-people parity, harvest storm, fear response, prayer duty, and anti-authoritarian safeguards.

verses.restored: Samuel said to all Israel, Look, I have listened to your voice in all you said to me, and I have set a king over you.
Now look, king walks before you. I am old and gray, and look, my sons are with you. I have walked before you from my youth to this day.

Here I am. Answer against me before YHWH and before his anointed: Whose ox have I taken? Whose donkey have I taken? Whom have I defrauded? Whom have I crushed? From whose hand have I taken a bribe to hide my eyes? I will restore it to you.
They said, You have not defrauded us, you have not crushed us, and you have not taken anything from hand of anyone.
He said to them, YHWH is witness against you, and his anointed is witness this day, that you have found nothing in my hand. They said, Witness.
Samuel said to people, YHWH is the one who appointed Moses and Aaron and brought your ancestors up from land of Egypt.
Now stand, and I will enter judgment with you before YHWH concerning all righteous acts of YHWH that he did with you and with your ancestors.
When Jacob came to Egypt and your ancestors cried to YHWH, YHWH sent Moses and Aaron, and they brought your ancestors out from Egypt and caused them to dwell in this place.
But they forgot YHWH their Divine, and he sold them into hand of Sisera commander of army of Hazor, into hand of Philistines, and into hand of king of Moab, and they fought against them.
They cried to YHWH and said, We have sinned, because we forsook YHWH and served Baals and Ashtaroth; now rescue us from hand of our enemies, and we will serve you.
YHWH sent Jerubbaal, Bedan, Jephthah, and Samuel, and rescued you from hand of enemies around you, and you dwelt securely.
When you saw Nahash king of children of Ammon coming against you, you said to me, No, but a king shall reign over us, though YHWH your Divine was your king.
Now look, king whom you chose and whom you asked for; look, YHWH has set a king over you.
If you fear YHWH, serve him, listen to his voice, and do not rebel against mouth of YHWH, then both you and king who reigns over you shall follow YHWH your Divine.
But if you do not listen to voice of YHWH and rebel against mouth of YHWH, hand of YHWH will be against you and against your ancestors/king, according to textual reading.
Now stand and see this great thing that YHWH will do before your eyes.
Is it not wheat harvest today? I will call to YHWH, and he will give thunder and rain, and you shall know and see that your evil is great, which you have done in eyes of YHWH by asking for a king.
Samuel called to YHWH, and YHWH gave thunder and rain that day. All people greatly feared YHWH and Samuel.
All people said to Samuel, Pray for your servants to YHWH your Divine, that we may not die, for we have added to all our sins the evil of asking for a king.
Samuel said to people, Do not fear. You have done all this evil; yet do not turn aside from following YHWH, and serve YHWH with all your heart.
Do not turn aside after empty things that cannot profit or rescue, because they are empty.
For YHWH will not abandon his people for sake of his great name, because YHWH was pleased to make you a people for himself.
Also as for me, far be it from me that I should sin against YHWH by ceasing to pray for you; I will instruct you in good and upright way.
Only fear YHWH and serve him in faithfulness with all your heart, for see what great things he has done with you.
But if doing evil you do evil, both you and your king will be swept away.

translation_notes: Samuel publicly submits his conduct to questions about property seizure, fraud, oppression, bribery, and restitution. Religious leaders require material accountability.
The people's affirmation concerns Samuel, not his sons; chapter 8's corruption complaint remains valid.
The historical review uses ancient covenant theology and cannot justify victim-blaming oppressed communities.
Bedan in verse 11 is textually difficult; witnesses and traditions vary, often linking the name with Barak.
King and people are placed under the same ethical demand. Anointing does not place rulers above law or prophetic critique.
The wheat-harvest storm functions as a sign within the narrative but may not justify terrorizing communities with weather or claiming disasters prove political sin.
The people's fear of death is answered with 'Do not fear' and continued belonging, not expulsion.
Prayer and instruction continue after political disagreement; spiritual care must not be withheld as punishment.
The final warning addresses both ruler and people, blocking leader worship and collective exemption from responsibility.
safety_note: Do not use this chapter to shame democratic participation, sanctify monarchy, terrorize people with weather, or make disasters evidence of Divine political judgment. Leaders must answer questions about theft, oppression, bribery, and restitution. Religious care may not be withheld from communities that make disputed choices.
